reasoning_content:
We are given a mutant: In `min_reduction_dim1_kernel`, line 24 of the original code is `if (col >= dim2) return;` which becomes `if (col > dim2) return;`. This is a boundary change from inclusive to exclusive. The original code uses `>=` to skip threads where `col` index is out of bounds; the mutant uses `>`. So threads with `col == dim2` will now pass the check and execute the kernel body.

We need to check if this can cause a difference under the fixed shape: input tensor shape [16,256,256], dtype float32. In the kernel launch for dim1 reduction: grid(batch_size,1,1) with block_size = min(dim2,1024). dim2=256, so block_size=256. Threads have indices col=0..255. dim2=256. In original, condition col >= 256 returns; all threads col=0..255 satisfy col < 256, so none return early (all threads are within bounds). In mutant, condition col > 256 returns; threads col=0..255 have col not > 256, so all threads also pass. So both conditions evaluate to false for all threads (original: col>=256 false for col<256; mutant: col>256 false for col<=255). So the condition is never true for any thread. Therefore the mutation does not change behavior at all for this fixed shape and launch configuration. The threads with col=256 do not exist because block_size=256, so no thread has col=256. So the boundary condition change is irrelevant because the thread index never reaches the boundary. The difference would only appear if block_size > dim2, then some threads would have col >= dim2. Here block_size = min(dim2,1024) = dim2 = 256, so all threads are within bounds. Thus the condition is always false for both original and mutant. The mutation is semantically identical under this fixed configuration. Therefore it is unkillable.

We must check other kernels as well? The mutation is only in dim1 kernel. The other kernels are unchanged. So overall the output will be identical for all inputs. The tests passed because indeed no difference can arise.

Thus reason_category: predicate_unreachable because the condition never triggers given fixed shape and launch parameters.

Proof sketch: For shape [16,256,256], dim2=256, block_size=min(256,1024)=256. Threads have col in [0,255]. Original condition col>=256 is false for all; mutant condition col>256 also false. So no thread is affected.

Survival reason: The mutation changes a boundary check from >= to >, but under the fixed shape (dim2=256) and launch parameters (block_size = dim2), no thread ever has col == dim2, so the condition always evaluates to false in both cases. Therefore the kernel behavior is identical for all inputs. All tests passed because the change is effectively dead code.

Killable: false.

Kill_strategy: Unkillable under this fixed shape contract because the modified condition never triggers any thread.

Suggested_test: null (since unkillable)

Recommendations: When testing mutations, ensure that boundary conditions are actually reachable given the launch configuration. Mutations on conditions that guard out-of-bounds but are never true for the fixed shape are harmless and survive due to lack of reachability. Consider adding additional shape configurations to tests, but the contract forbids shape changes. So this mutant is unkillable under the given contract.
